1.9: Diotrephes 'who likes to put himself first' (philoproteuo, v. 9) represents the emergence of local congregational autonomy that resisted the authority of the itinerant apostolic elder. The conflict between the elder's authority (rooted in the apostolic tradition) and Diotrephes's local authority (rooted in congregational control) anticipates the later tension between episcopal and local governance structures. Diotrephes's practices — refusing the elder's letter, refusing to receive traveling brothers, and expelling those who do — constitute a miniature local church coup. 3 John is the NT's only document addressing intra-Christian church politics at this granular level.
1.1: 'The elder to the beloved Gaius, whom I love in truth' — the self-designation 'elder' (ho presbyteros) is the same as in 2 John 1 and identifies the author as the Johannine elder — in early church tradition identified with John the apostle writing from Ephesus in his old age. Gaius was one of the most common names in the Roman world; the three other NT figures named Gaius (Acts 19:29; 20:4; Rom 16:23; 1 Cor 1:14) are likely different individuals. The letter is the only NT epistle addressed to a named private individual other than Philemon. 'Whom I love in truth' (hon agapō en alētheia) — as in 2 John, the love is grounded in the truth that is the defining reality of the Johannine community.
1.2: 'Beloved, I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health, just as it is going well with your soul' — the health-wish (euche... hygiainein) is a standard Hellenistic letter formula; examples abound in Greek papyri: 'I pray that you are in good health, for I myself am well.' The formula appears in Polycarp's letters to Philippi and in other early Christian correspondence. The elder Christianizes it by adding 'just as your soul prospers' (kathōs euodoutai sou hē psychē) — the standard social courtesy is subordinated to a higher register of wellbeing; spiritual flourishing is the benchmark against which physical health is measured.
1.3: 'For I rejoiced greatly when the brothers came and testified to your truth, as indeed you are walking in truth' — the itinerant brothers who reported on Gaius are the early church's network of traveling teachers and messengers who served as the primary information conduit between scattered house-church communities. Their oral testimony (martyrountōn) about Gaius's 'truth' (alētheia sou) — his faithfulness to the apostolic teaching — parallels the role of Epaphras in Col 1:8 (who reported the Colossians' love to Paul). In a world without fast communication, itinerant messengers carried community news, financial support, and apostolic authority between churches.
1.4: 'I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth' — the 'children' (ta ema tekna) is apostolic language: Paul uses the same phrase for his converts (1 Cor 4:14: my beloved children; Gal 4:19: my little children). The elder regards those he has formed in the apostolic tradition as his own spiritual offspring. The 'greater joy' (meizoteran... charan) is the relational payoff of apostolic ministry: news of continued faithfulness among those who have been formed in truth is worth more than any other report. Ignatius of Antioch expresses the same pastoral joy in his letters to communities walking faithfully (Ignatius, Ephesians 1; Philippians 5).
1.5: 'Beloved, it is a faithful thing you do in all your efforts for the brothers and sisters, especially for strangers' — the hospitality that Gaius extended to the traveling brothers is the ancient church's vital support system. The Greek ethical ideal of xenia (hospitality to strangers) and the Jewish mitzvah of hachnasat orchim (welcoming guests) both feed into the early Christian practice of providing lodging, food, and travel provisions for missionaries. The Didache (11-13) regulates exactly this practice: itinerant apostles and teachers are to be received like the Lord himself, given hospitality for one or two days, then sent on with provisions. Gaius's welcome of 'strangers' (xenous) who turned out to be brothers is this system functioning as intended.
1.6: 'They have borne witness to your love before the church. You will do well to send them on their journey in a manner worthy of God' — 'send them on their way' (propempsas) is a technical term in early Christianity for the act of provisioning and providing travel assistance to missionaries departing for their next destination (Acts 15:3; 20:38; 21:5; Rom 15:24; 1 Cor 16:6, 11; 2 Cor 1:16; Titus 3:13). It involved more than saying goodbye: providing food, money, escort, letters of introduction, and practical supplies. 'In a manner worthy of God' (axiōs tou theou) echoes 1 Thess 2:12 (walk in a manner worthy of God who calls you) — the hospitality is calibrated not to social custom but to the character of God himself.
1.7: 'For they have gone out for the sake of the Name, accepting nothing from the Gentiles' — 'the Name' (hyper tou onomatos) is the divine Name of Jesus — the basis and content of the entire mission (Acts 5:41: rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the Name; 3 John 7 is the only NT occurrence of the absolute 'the Name' as shorthand for Jesus without additional specification). The policy of 'accepting nothing from the Gentiles' (mēden lambanontes apo tōn ethnōn) reflects a deliberate missionary strategy: some workers refused financial support from non-believers to avoid any appearance that the gospel was being sold or that the missionaries were like the wandering Sophist philosophers who charged fees. Paul adopted a similar policy in Corinth (1 Cor 9:12) while accepting support from Philippi (Phil 4:15-16).
1.8: 'Therefore we ought to support such people, so that we may be fellow workers for the truth' — the theological ground for hospitality: by supporting missionaries, the home community becomes 'co-workers for the truth' (synergoi tē alētheia). The same logic appears in Phil 4:17-18: Paul speaks of the Philippians' financial gift as fruit that accrues to their account — the support-giver participates in the missionary's work and shares in its fruit. The Didache develops this into a principle: 'Every true prophet who wants to live with you is worthy of his food' (13.1); the community that supports the prophet participates in the prophetic mission. Gaius's hospitality is not charity but apostolic partnership.
1.10: 'Therefore, if I come, I will call attention to what he is doing, spreading wicked nonsense against us. And not content with that, he refuses to welcome the brothers, and also stops those who want to welcome them and puts them out of the church' — Diotrephes's offenses are enumerated in escalating severity: (1) phlya rōn (babbling malicious nonsense) — verbal attack on the elder's authority, probably spreading rumors that undermined the elder's letters and messengers; (2) refusing personal hospitality to the brothers; (3) actively blocking others who want to extend hospitality; (4) excommunicating (ekballei) those who do welcome the brothers. The last offense is the most serious: Diotrephes is exercising a disciplinary authority that belongs to the whole community (or the apostolic leadership), using it to punish theological opponents. Comparable disputes over local versus itinerant authority appear in the Didache (11.1-12) and the letters of Ignatius (who argues for episcopal authority against schism).
1.11: 'Beloved, do not imitate evil but imitate good. The one doing good is from God; the one doing evil has not seen God' — the direct paraenetic contrast drawn from the Diotrephes (evil) / Demetrius (good) case study. The language of imitation (mimou) is the ancient method of moral formation: character is developed by imitating exemplars, both negative (avoid) and positive (follow). The Pauline letters use this pattern extensively (1 Cor 4:16; 11:1; Phil 3:17; 1 Thess 1:6). The theological ground — 'from God' vs. 'has not seen God' — recalls 1 John's characteristic epistemology: authentic community belonging is demonstrated by ethical life; those who truly know God act accordingly, and those who act wickedly demonstrate that they have never truly known him (1 John 4:8: whoever does not love does not know God).
1.12: 'Demetrius has received a good testimony from everyone, and from the truth itself. We also add our testimony, and you know that our testimony is true' — Demetrius is commended in three registers: (1) hypo pantōn (by everyone) — universal community regard; (2) hypo autēs tēs alētheias (by the truth itself) — an unusual phrase that may mean his life so conforms to the apostolic truth that the teaching itself vouches for him, or that the Truth-community (the Johannine tradition) endorses him; (3) the elder's own personal testimony. The triple witness structure echoes Deut 19:15 (a matter is established by two or three witnesses) and 2 Cor 13:1 (Paul's citation of the same principle). Demetrius may have been the letter-carrier who delivered 3 John to Gaius, which would explain the personal commendation.
1.13: 'I have much to write to you, but I do not want to write to you with pen and ink' — the same formula as 2 John 12 (pollā echōn graphein soi, ouk ēboulēthēn dia chartou kai melanos, using 'paper and ink' for 2 John vs. 'pen and ink' here). In the ancient world, correspondence was expensive and cumbersome: papyrus or parchment, ink, sealing wax, and a messenger all had to be arranged. Many important matters were reserved for personal visit rather than correspondence, as letters were public documents read aloud to the assembled community — private and sensitive matters were better handled face to face. The brevity of 3 John (245 Greek words) reflects exactly this: the letter conveys only what must be written now; the fuller conversation waits.
1.14: 'I hope to see you soon, and we will speak face to face. Peace to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends, each by name' — the wish for face-to-face (stoma pros stoma) encounter (as in 2 John 12) reflects the ancient valuation of personal presence as superior to written communication (Plato, Phaedrus 275c-276a: writing cannot substitute for living dialogue; Seneca, Letters 1.1: be with people; Papyri: 'I pray to see your face'). The 'peace to you' (eirēnē soi) is the Semitic shalom applied as the Christian benediction. The greeting 'by name' (kat onoma) — each individual friend named personally — reflects the intimate scale of the house-church communities: the elder knows each member of Gaius's community by name, as a shepherd knows each sheep (John 10:3: he calls his own sheep by name).
